exercises.title: Fill Missing Values
exercises.description: Implement three missing-value fill strategies on a list of dicts: fill with a constant, fill with the column mean, and fill with the column mode (most frequent value). Test on a dataset with missing ages, salaries, and departments. Print before and after counts.
exercises.starterCode:
from collections import Counter

def fill_with_constant(records, col, value):
    """Fill None values in col with a constant value. Returns new list."""
    # TODO: Return a new list where records with None in col are filled
    pass

def fill_with_mean(records, col):
    """Fill None values in col with the column mean (numeric only). Returns new list."""
    # TODO: Calculate mean of non-None values
    # TODO: Return new list with Nones replaced by the rounded mean (1 decimal)
    pass

def fill_with_mode(records, col):
    """Fill None values in col with the most frequent non-None value. Returns new list."""
    # TODO: Find the most common non-None value using Counter
    # TODO: Return new list with Nones replaced
    pass

employees = [
    {"name": "Alice",  "age": 28,   "salary": 75000, "dept": "Engineering"},
    {"name": "Bob",    "age": None, "salary": 55000, "dept": "Marketing"},
    {"name": "Carol",  "age": 34,   "salary": None,  "dept": "Engineering"},
    {"name": "Dave",   "age": 29,   "salary": 62000, "dept": None},
    {"name": "Eve",    "age": None, "salary": 88000, "dept": "Engineering"},
    {"name": "Frank",  "age": 25,   "salary": None,  "dept": "Marketing"},
]

null_before = sum(1 for r in employees for v in r.values() if v is None)
print(f"Nulls before: {null_before}")

# Apply fills
result = fill_with_mean(employees, "age")
result = fill_with_mean(result, "salary")
result = fill_with_mode(result, "dept")

null_after = sum(1 for r in result for v in r.values() if v is None)
print(f"Nulls after: {null_after}")

# Print filled values
for r in result:
    print(f"{r['name']:<8} age={r['age']} salary={r['salary']} dept={r['dept']}")
exercises.expectedOutput:
Nulls before: 4
Nulls after: 0
Alice    age=28 salary=75000 dept=Engineering
Bob      age=29.0 salary=55000 dept=Marketing
Carol    age=34 salary=62500.0 dept=Engineering
Dave     age=29 salary=62000 dept=Engineering
Eve      age=29.0 salary=88000 dept=Engineering
Frank    age=25 salary=62500.0 dept=Marketing
hints: fill_with_constant: [{**r, col: value} if r[col] is None else r for r in records]
fill_with_mean: mean = round(sum(v for v in non_null) / len(non_null), 1)
Use dict unpacking to create a new dict with one field changed: {**r, col: fill_val}

exercises.title: String Cleaning Pipeline
exercises.description: Clean a messy string dataset. Write a `clean_name(s)` function that: strips whitespace, converts to title case, removes non-alphanumeric characters except spaces, and collapses multiple spaces. Also write `clean_email(s)` that strips, lowercases, and validates format. Apply both to messy data.
exercises.starterCode:
import re

def clean_name(s):
    """
    Clean a name string:
    1. Strip leading/trailing whitespace
    2. Remove characters that aren't letters, spaces, or hyphens
    3. Collapse multiple spaces into one
    4. Convert to title case
    """

        return None
    # TODO: Implement the 4 steps above
    pass

def clean_email(s):
    """
    Clean an email string:
    1. Strip whitespace
    2. Lowercase
    3. Return None if '@' not in email or '.' not in domain part
    """
    if s is None:
        return None
    # TODO: Implement cleaning and validation
    pass

messy_data = [
    {"name": "  alice   smith  ",   "email": "  Alice@Example.COM  "},
    {"name": "BOB_JONES123",        "email": "bob@test.com"},
    {"name": "  carol  O'Brien",   "email": "CAROL@COMPANY.ORG"},
    {"name": "dave   wilson",       "email": "dave-at-example.com"},  # invalid email
    {"name": "Eve!!!  Nguyen",      "email": "eve@email.co"},
]

print("Cleaned data:")
for row in messy_data:
    cleaned_name = clean_name(row["name"])
    cleaned_email = clean_email(row["email"])
    print(f"  Name:  '{cleaned_name}'")
    print(f"  Email: {cleaned_email}")
exercises.expectedOutput:
Cleaned data:
  Name:  'Alice Smith'
  Email: alice@example.com
  Name:  'Bobjones'
  Email: bob@test.com
  Name:  "Carol O'Brien"
  Email: carol@company.org
  Name:  'Dave Wilson'
  Email: None
  Name:  'Eve Nguyen'
  Email: eve@email.co
hints: clean_name step 2: s = re.sub(r"[^a-zA-Z\s'-]", '', s) — keep letters, spaces, hyphens, apostrophes
clean_email validation: check '@' in email and '.' in email.split('@')[1]

exercises.title: Outlier Detection with IQR
exercises.description: Implement the IQR outlier detection method. Write `detect_outliers(data)` that computes Q1, Q3, IQR, and identifies values below Q1 - 1.5*IQR or above Q3 + 1.5*IQR. Also write `remove_outliers(data)` and `cap_outliers(data)` (winsorize). Test on a salary dataset.
exercises.starterCode:
def quartile(data, q):
    """Compute the q-th quartile (0.25 or 0.75) of a sorted list."""
    sorted_data = sorted(data)
    n = len(sorted_data)
    idx = q * (n - 1)
    lower = int(idx)
    upper = lower + 1
    if upper >= n:
        return sorted_data[lower]
    frac = idx - lower
    return sorted_data[lower] + frac * (sorted_data[upper] - sorted_data[lower])

def detect_outliers(data):
    """Return (outliers, lower_bound, upper_bound) using IQR method."""
    # TODO: Compute Q1, Q3, IQR
    # TODO: Compute lower = Q1 - 1.5*IQR, upper = Q3 + 1.5*IQR
    # TODO: Return (list of outlier values, lower, upper)
    pass

def remove_outliers(data):
    """Return data with outliers removed."""
    _, lower, upper = detect_outliers(data)
    # TODO: Filter out values outside [lower, upper]
    pass

def cap_outliers(data):
    """Return data with outliers capped at the bounds (winsorize)."""
    _, lower, upper = detect_outliers(data)
    # TODO: Replace values below lower with lower, above upper with upper
    pass

salaries = [45000, 52000, 61000, 48000, 55000, 59000, 200000, 51000, 47000, 53000, 1000, 58000]

outliers, lower, upper = detect_outliers(salaries)
print(f"Q1-1.5*IQR lower bound: {lower:.0f}")
print(f"Q3+1.5*IQR upper bound: {upper:.0f}")
print(f"Outliers found: {sorted(outliers)}")

cleaned = remove_outliers(salaries)
print(f"\nAfter removing outliers:")
print(f"Count: {len(cleaned)} (was {len(salaries)})")
print(f"Mean: {sum(cleaned)/len(cleaned):.0f}")

capped = cap_outliers(salaries)
print(f"\nAfter capping outliers:")
print(f"Min: {min(capped):.0f}  Max: {max(capped):.0f}")
exercises.expectedOutput:
Q1-1.5*IQR lower bound: 37375
Q3+1.5*IQR upper bound: 72625
Outliers found: [1000, 200000]

After removing outliers:
Count: 10 (was 12)
Mean: 52900

After capping outliers:
Min: 37375  Max: 72625
hints: Q1 = quartile(data, 0.25); Q3 = quartile(data, 0.75); IQR = Q3 - Q1
lower = Q1 - 1.5 * IQR; upper = Q3 + 1.5 * IQR
outliers = [x for x in data if x < lower or x > upper]
cap: [max(lower, min(upper, x)) for x in data]

warmup.title: Quick Recall: Sorting and Median
warmup.starterCode:
data = [9, 3, 7, 1, 5]
sorted_data = sorted(data)
n = len(sorted_data)
median = sorted_data[n // 2]
print(sorted_data)
print(f"Median: {median}")
